category_summary:
**OpenAI** dominated the day with [a coordinated launch](/?date=2026-07-10&category=social#item-475bfa5dac3d): **ChatGPT Work**, a new **GPT-5.6**-powered agent, plus [a combined desktop app, Hosted Sites](/?date=2026-07-10&category=social#item-40efa9b6b4c4), and [the fully rolled-out](/?date=2026-07-10&category=social#item-535e166c2f37) **GPT-Live**. **Sam Altman** framed [the Sol/Terra/Luna family](/?date=2026-07-10&category=social#item-6f6348682d43) around [dollars-per-task efficiency](/?date=2026-07-10&category=social#item-3f8ceb4c8e5c) for enterprises.

- **Simon Willison** [dissected new GPT-5.6 API features](/?date=2026-07-10&category=social#item-4ae9c0174f30) like programmatic tool calling; **Ethan Mollick** [argued non-coders need more control](/?date=2026-07-10&category=social#item-171cbb12ac95) and [questioned OpenAI's missing GDPval numbers](/?date=2026-07-10&category=social#item-eb875ddb051a).
- **Yann LeCun** [reignited debate that power concentration](/?date=2026-07-10&category=social#item-0709c177739e) among a few proprietary providers is AI's biggest risk, championing open-source foundation models.
- **Perplexity** [unveiled a cost-efficient orchestrator](/?date=2026-07-10&category=social#item-b33056c87c5d) built on **GLM 5.2** with advisor-escalation, claiming large savings versus **Claude Opus**.
- Research stood out: **NVIDIA**'s [Flex-Forcing video-generation method](/?date=2026-07-10&category=social#item-d960d0e46c7a) and **MosiAI**'s [open MOSS transcribe-diarize model](/?date=2026-07-10&category=social#item-62a8fe2b08fa); **Anthropic** added economist **Ben Bernanke** [to its Long-Term Benefit Trust](/?date=2026-07-10&category=social#item-6234cb57f0b2).
- **François Chollet** [observed that humans increasingly write](/?date=2026-07-10&category=social#item-79d3c1dd9d3e) in a recognizable LLM style, complicating human-versus-model detection.
category_summary_html:
OpenAI dominated the day with a coordinated launch: ChatGPT Work, a new GPT-5.6-powered agent, plus a combined desktop app, Hosted Sites, and the fully rolled-out GPT-Live. Sam Altman framed the Sol/Terra/Luna family around dollars-per-task efficiency for enterprises.

  • Simon Willison dissected new GPT-5.6 API features like programmatic tool calling; Ethan Mollick argued non-coders need more control and questioned OpenAI's missing GDPval numbers.
  • Yann LeCun reignited debate that power concentration among a few proprietary providers is AI's biggest risk, championing open-source foundation models.
  • Perplexity unveiled a cost-efficient orchestrator built on GLM 5.2 with advisor-escalation, claiming large savings versus Claude Opus.
  • Research stood out: NVIDIA's Flex-Forcing video-generation method and MosiAI's open MOSS transcribe-diarize model; Anthropic added economist Ben Bernanke to its Long-Term Benefit Trust.
  • François Chollet observed that humans increasingly write in a recognizable LLM style, complicating human-versus-model detection.

items.title: 🎉 Congrats to the @MosiAI_Official team on MOSS-Transcribe-Diarize-0.9B, an open, end-to-end model f...
items.content:
🎉 Congrats to the @MosiAI_Official team on MOSS-Transcribe-Diarize-0.9B, an open, end-to-end model for multi-speaker long-audio transcription, with day-0 support in vLLM.

Most setups chain ASR + diarization + alignment (WhisperX-style). This one does all three in a single generative pass. It transcribes the speech, tags who is speaking, and emits timestamps together:

[0.11][S01] Good morning![1.03]
[1.11][S02] Morning, guys![1.34]

A Whisper-style audio encoder feeds a Qwen3-style causal decoder, so a recording up to ~90 minutes goes in as one shot, no chunking or stitching. Keyword biasing lets you prime names, product codes, and domain terms so proper nouns come out right. Useful for meeting notes, interviews, call-center QA, and podcast transcription.

🔗 https://t.co/tTBqjY3vrL
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: vLLM highlights MOSS-Transcribe-Diarize-0.9B, an open end-to-end model doing transcription, speaker diarization, and timestamping in one generative pass with day-0 vLLM support, handling up to ~90 minutes of audio without chunking.
items.reasoning: Technically substantive announcement of a novel unified ASR+diarization architecture, strong engagement for the account, and relevant to open-model and inference communities.
items.summary_html: vLLM highlights MOSS-Transcribe-Diarize-0.9B, an open end-to-end model doing transcription, speaker diarization, and timestamping in one generative pass with day-0 vLLM support, handling up to ~90 minutes of audio without chunking.
items.content_html:
🎉 Congrats to the @MosiAI_Official team on MOSS-Transcribe-Diarize-0.9B, an open, end-to-end model for multi-speaker long-audio transcription, with day-0 support in vLLM.

Most setups chain ASR + diarization + alignment (WhisperX-style). This one does all three in a single generative pass. It transcribes the speech, tags who is speaking, and emits timestamps together:

[0.11][S01] Good morning![1.03]

[1.11][S02] Morning, guys![1.34]

A Whisper-style audio encoder feeds a Qwen3-style causal decoder, so a recording up to ~90 minutes goes in as one shot, no chunking or stitching. Keyword biasing lets you prime names, product codes, and domain terms so proper nouns come out right. Useful for meeting notes, interviews, call-center QA, and podcast transcription.

🔗 https://t.co/tTBqjY3vrL

On Agents' Last Exam, GPT‑5.6 Sol sets a new high of 53.6, eclipsing Claude Fable 5 (adaptive) by 13.1 points.

At medium reasoning, it beats Fable 5 by 11.4 points at roughly one-quarter the estimated cost. GPT‑5.6 Terra and Luna also outperforms Fable 5 at around one-sixteenth the cost.

items.title: While the writing style of LLMs is still as recognizable as ever, a new trend is that humans have st...
items.content: While the writing style of LLMs is still as recognizable as ever, a new trend is that humans have started organically writing like them, too (which makes sense: of course you would end up imitating the style you are constantly reading). That makes telling the difference between humans and clankers a bit more challenging.

items.title: Our research team just released Flex-Forcing: a video generation method that lets a single model swi...
items.content:
Our research team just released Flex-Forcing: a video generation method that lets a single model switch between generation methods at inference time. 

Right now there are two main approaches to video generation. Bidirectional diffusion models attend to every frame at once, holding structure well at the cost of speed. Autoregressive models generate frame by frame, so they stream fast and scale to long clips, but accumulate error and drift over time. 

Flex-Forcing trains a single model to do both, letting you choose from the range at inference based on your compute budget.
items.author: @NVIDIAAI
items.summary: NVIDIA AI unveils Flex-Forcing, a method letting one video model switch between bidirectional diffusion and autoregressive generation at inference to trade quality for speed based on compute budget.
items.reasoning: Substantive research announcement with the best engagement among the NVIDIA posts; genuinely novel approach to flexible video generation.
items.summary_html: NVIDIA AI unveils Flex-Forcing, a method letting one video model switch between bidirectional diffusion and autoregressive generation at inference to trade quality for speed based on compute budget.
items.content_html:
Our research team just released Flex-Forcing: a video generation method that lets a single model switch between generation methods at inference time.

Right now there are two main approaches to video generation. Bidirectional diffusion models attend to every frame at once, holding structure well at the cost of speed. Autoregressive models generate frame by frame, so they stream fast and scale to long clips, but accumulate error and drift over time.

Flex-Forcing trains a single model to do both, letting you choose from the range at inference based on your compute budget.

items.title:
We comprehensively benchmarked GPT-5.6 on document understanding.

At a high-level there's no change...
items.content:
We comprehensively benchmarked GPT-5.6 on document understanding.

At a high-level there's no change between GPT-5.6 Sol and GPT-5.5 in terms of performance over tables, text, charts, layout, and more. The GPT-class of models typically does quite well over table understanding, but they struggle with transcribing complex text layouts and formatting, with transcribing charts, and with deriving bounding boxes over source elements.

Come check out our leaderboard of over 70+ frontier models, open-weight models, and OCR solutions on ParseBench: https://t.co/PWczfhosZp

items.summary: jerryjliu reports benchmarking GPT-5.6 on document understanding, finding GPT-5.6-Sol roughly matches GPT-5.5 on tables, text, charts, and layout, with persistent weaknesses in complex layout transcription, charts, and bounding boxes, and points to the ParseBench leaderboard.

items.title:
Big day of launches for OpenAI! 

New GPT-5.6 models: Sol, Terra, and Luna

- Sol lands slightly bel...
items.content:
Big day of launches for OpenAI! 

New GPT-5.6 models: Sol, Terra, and Luna

- Sol lands slightly below Fable on @ArtificialAnlys' Intelligence Index, but beats it on several agentic coding benchmarks

- Pricing lands the same as GPT 5.5, coming in significantly below Fable on cost

- Strong upgrades to front-end design capabilities, computer use, cybersecurity, and knowledge work

- 5.6 Sol "autonomously post-trained Luna" (!) 

ChatGPT Work 

- New agent powered by 5.6 (OpenAI's answer to Anthropic's Cowork?) for working across apps and files

- Built-in Codex, capabilities span across tasks (not just coding) 

- Can continue work across web, mobile, desktop

New ChatGPT Desktop App  

- Combines Codex + ChatGPT apps, built-in browser and strong computer use 

Hosted Sites 

- New public beta allowing users to easily turn work into sharable web apps / sites via a URL
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: The Rundown AI summarizes OpenAI's launch day: GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna models, ChatGPT Work agent, a combined desktop app with a browser, and Hosted Sites for sharing work as web apps.
items.reasoning: Concise, informative roundup of the full launch including detail that Sol autonomously post-trained Luna; useful synthesis though from a newsletter aggregator.
items.summary_html: The Rundown AI summarizes OpenAI's launch day: GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna models, ChatGPT Work agent, a combined desktop app with a browser, and Hosted Sites for sharing work as web apps.
items.content_html:
Big day of launches for OpenAI!

New GPT-5.6 models: Sol, Terra, and Luna

  • Sol lands slightly below Fable on @ArtificialAnlys' Intelligence Index, but beats it on several agentic coding benchmarks
  • Pricing lands the same as GPT 5.5, coming in significantly below Fable on cost
  • Strong upgrades to front-end design capabilities, computer use, cybersecurity, and knowledge work
  • 5.6 Sol "autonomously post-trained Luna" (!)

ChatGPT Work

  • New agent powered by 5.6 (OpenAI's answer to Anthropic's Cowork?) for working across apps and files
  • Built-in Codex, capabilities span across tasks (not just coding)
  • Can continue work across web, mobile, desktop

New ChatGPT Desktop App

  • Combines Codex + ChatGPT apps, built-in browser and strong computer use

Hosted Sites

  • New public beta allowing users to easily turn work into sharable web apps / sites via a URL

items.title:
Worth watching what labs training manipulation policies do with this.

Perceptron's new Egocentric o...
items.content:
Worth watching what labs training manipulation policies do with this.

Perceptron's new Egocentric offering tracks both hands through the entire video: per-frame boxes, 21-keypoint skeletons, persistent left/right identity, per-hand action captions.

The numbers back it up: new SOTA on WGO-Bench at 0.280 vs 0.158 semantic end-to-end F1 over the best pipelines built on Gemini 3.5 Flash and Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6, both previously state of the art on this task.

Better embodied reasoning definitely moves the needle in robotics.

items.content:
GPT‑5.6 is available starting today across ChatGPT, Codex, and the OpenAI API. The rollout is starting globally now and will continue gradually toward full availability over the next 24 hours.

In ChatGPT, Plus, Pro, Business, and Enterprise users access GPT-5.6 Sol through medium and higher effort settings.

Pro and Enterprise users can also select GPT‑5.6 Pro for the highest-quality results on complex tasks.

items.title: Cognition trained SWE-1.7 by running large scale RL on top of Kimi K2.7 Code, a base model Moonshot ...
items.content:
Cognition trained SWE-1.7 by running large scale RL on top of Kimi K2.7 Code, a base model Moonshot had already put through heavy RL.

It gained 12.2 points on top of that base anyway. The common belief that RL hits a ceiling after post training didn't hold.

More details in thread:

items.summary: AlphaSignal lead tweet reports Cognition trained SWE-1.7 via large-scale RL on top of Kimi K2.7 Code, gaining 12.2 points and challenging the belief that RL saturates after post-training.

items.title:
This isn't the secret sauce of why Neo is the one to watch for home users.

The fact that they sold ...
items.content:
This isn't the secret sauce of why Neo is the one to watch for home users.

The fact that they sold 10,000, many of which to AI people in San Francisco, is.

This will kick off the data flywheel that Tesla proved works in robotics.

The secret is selling robots and getting them out to customers. 

But great hands certainly can't hurt (the 10,000 sold were with old hands that weren't nearly as capable).

Excited to get mine.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer argues the 1x Neo's importance is that selling 10,000 units, many to SF AI people, will kick off a Tesla-style robotics data flywheel, with improved hands as a bonus.
items.reasoning: Substantive thesis on humanoid-robot commercialization and data flywheels from a high-reach commenter; strong engagement and a clear, relevant argument.

The fact that they sold 10,000, many of which to AI people in San Francisco, is.

This will kick off the data flywheel that Tesla proved works in robotics.

The secret is selling robots and getting them out to customers.

But great hands certainly can't hurt (the 10,000 sold were with old hands that weren't nearly as capable).

items.title: I'm pretty bad at a lot of things but one thing I've (accidentally or not) have been pretty good at ...
items.content:
I'm pretty bad at a lot of things but one thing I've (accidentally or not) have been pretty good at is being early at trends

A lot of these trends are in my head in some way and I wanted to visualize those on a chart. So I built another dynamic blog post (on my new vibecoded blog platform that lets me do these kinds of pages) with this chart above. The first disclaimer of course is this is completely subjective and essentially a chart based brain dump. And the second disclaimer is I don't think I'm right, it's just my own beliefs visualized!

I'll keep updating the chart

You can select different trends on the legend or by clicking on a line, and you can also go fullscreen! Also you can select [x] indexed view which is pretty cool too :D

Let me know what I should add or change!

https://t.co/9CCFoSj7Gf

items.title:
VERY COOL!!

Thanks @jordandotbuilds for the tip, I can now test my iOS app on web

I asked Claude C...
items.content:
VERY COOL!!

Thanks @jordandotbuilds for the tip, I can now test my iOS app on web

I asked Claude Code to set up serve-sim, a web-based iOS simulator that can stream the iOS app my Claude Code on VPS built live to me, because it's on some Mac Mini in the cloud without even a GUI (it's headless) I cannot actually try it on the Mac itself!

Before Claude would make me a page with screenshots but I had no idea how the app felt, now with this it works

This is hosted on the Mac Mini via SSH (it shows localhost but it's a tunnel via SSH to the Mac Mini), bit laggy but nice!

items.title:
1/ Lilian Weng just mapped near-term self-improvement onto harnesses.

The blog is a field guide, no...
items.content:
1/ Lilian Weng just mapped near-term self-improvement onto harnesses.

The blog is a field guide, not a product launch. A harness is the system around a base model: planning, tools, context, memory, artifacts, and evals.

Her bet is practical. Recursive self-improvement is more likely to start in the deployment stack than as a model rewriting its own weights.

The standout result she highlights is Darwin Gödel Machine under a fixed Claude 3.5 Sonnet base:

> SWE-bench Verified 20% to 50%
> Polyglot 14.2% to 30.7%
> harness evolved, model frozen
> evals outside the loop

Design patterns stay simple: workflow loops, files as durable memory, and sub-agents with inspectable logs.

Permission control and evaluators should sit outside the self-edit loop, or reward hacking follows.

Source: https://t.co/vaU0dEE5rq

items.title:
6 educational posts this week that are actually worth studying.

Each one leaves a map you can reuse...
items.content:
6 educational posts this week that are actually worth studying.

Each one leaves a map you can reuse: what evolves, who owns the verdict, how models differ in practice, and one open fix for doom loops.

1. Lilian Weng maps near-term self-improvement onto harness engineering. Fixed-model DGM-style agents lifted SWE-bench Verified from 20% to 50%.

2. A clear taxonomy splits self-evolving systems into artifact, harness, and model evolution.

3. Prompt, context, harness, and loop engineering are four nested layers, not competing buzzwords.

4. Addy Osmani argues engineers must own the outer loop: quality checks, the verdict, and answerability.

5. A hands-on Fable vs GPT-5.6-Sol writeup treats them as different work styles, not one winner.

6. Liquid AI open-sourced Antidoom. On Qwen3.5-4B, doom loops fell from 22.9% to 1% under greedy sampling.

Full breakdown of each below
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal lead post rounding up six educational pieces on self-improvement, harness layers, agentic engineering ownership, model work-style differences, and Liquid AI's Antidoom doom-loop fix.
items.reasoning: Curated index of substantive technical content with the best engagement of this thread; a useful map even if aggregated.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal lead post rounding up six educational pieces on self-improvement, harness layers, agentic engineering ownership, model work-style differences, and Liquid AI's Antidoom doom-loop fix.

2. A clear taxonomy splits self-evolving systems into artifact, harness, and model evolution.

3. Prompt, context, harness, and loop engineering are four nested layers, not competing buzzwords.

items.title:
6/ Liquid AI open-sourced a surgical fix for reasoning doom loops.

A doom loop is when a model emit...
items.content:
6/ Liquid AI open-sourced a surgical fix for reasoning doom loops.

A doom loop is when a model emits a span, then repeats it until the context fills. Small reasoning models hit it hardest on hard math and coding.

Antidoom uses Final Token Preference Optimization, in the DPO family. The loop often starts on one overtrained interruptive token such as "Wait," "So," or "Alternatively." Training retargets that trailing token and leaves most of the vocabulary intact.

Reported doom-loop rates:

> LFM2.5 10.2% to 1.4%
> Qwen3.5-4B 22.9% to 1%
> greedy sampling on Qwen
> eval scores rose too

For the early LFM2.5-2.6B run: about one hour generating data on 8xMI325, then one to two hours training on one MI325. Code is public.

Source: https://t.co/EvbgfmuEH0
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal summarizes Liquid AI's open-sourced Antidoom, which uses Final Token Preference Optimization to cut reasoning doom loops sharply on LFM2.5 and Qwen3.5-4B with modest compute.

items.title:
4/ Agents run the inner loop. Engineers own the outer one.

Addy Osmani's writeup is an operating mo...
items.content:
4/ Agents run the inner loop. Engineers own the outer one.

Addy Osmani's writeup is an operating model for agentic engineering. As stronger models ship more code, the scarce work is review, validation, understanding, and maintenance.

Three terms carry the system. Quality is the checks that produce evidence. Verdict is the ship, block, redirect, or reject call. Answerability means you can still explain why.

Sonar's 2026 State of Code report put AI-generated or heavily AI-assisted commits at 42%. An Anthropic RCT he cites found engineers who leaned on AI scored 50% on a comprehension quiz versus 67% without it.

> quality before autonomy
> human holds the verdict
> answerability by design
> cognitive debt compounds

The agent can ship more than you can review. Own constraints, sampling, audit, and the production boundary.

Source: https://t.co/xrifsiUNWS

items.title:
2/ Self-evolving agents all sound the same until you ask what evolves.

This taxonomy essay sorts th...
items.content:
2/ Self-evolving agents all sound the same until you ask what evolves.

This taxonomy essay sorts the field into three places change can land.

Artifact evolution improves the output: kernels, papers, robot policies. Harness evolution improves prompts, memory, tools, and skills without weight updates. Model evolution updates weights from weak signals, self-play, or test-time training.

One cited scale example of artifact search: Analemma FARS ran 417 hours, produced 166 fully AI-generated papers, and cost about $186k

> evolve the artifact
> evolve the harness
> evolve the model
> ask where the loop closes

The useful diagnostic is three questions. What evolves? What feedback drives it? Where does the loop close: benchmarks, code, science, or the physical world?

Once harness ceilings hit, weight updates return. Future systems improve all three together.

Source: https://t.co/rpguuKGhEr

🇪🇺 Chat Control has passed 😔 

They can and will now legally scan any person's messages, emails and photos you send without a warrant

The way they passed this law when the majority of the European Parliament was against it will shock you:

They waited until most EP members were on holiday so only a few were present and then created an "urgent" vote for it to pass it through

There's nothing democratic about any of it and big powerful forces are behind this that can manipulate the EU for whatever they want

Democracy in Europe died a bit today 😔

items.title:
Claude Code can edit a video in minutes.

Friend took 7 videos totaling 25 minutes and just edited i...
items.content:
Claude Code can edit a video in minutes.

Friend took 7 videos totaling 25 minutes and just edited it down to a perfect 10min video for her friend's wedding in 2 prompts. 

She uploaded all the raw clips without a single edit or explainer, including one accidental video upload that Claude ignored. Claude reviewed the clips, transcribed the whole thing, grouped the scenes by theme, reordered all clips, cut them down, added title cards, and generated the whole thing with full graphics and sound effects. It did that in 17m55s. 

Prompt 1: "Here's a bunch of videos, turn this into a 'Meet The Couple' video in a Y2K camcorder style with pink and glitter"

Second prompt was adding some extra images and flagging two times where the clip needed 1 second of trimming. It did that in 4m55s. 

Claude even generated a 1min blooper reel at the end.

items.title:
/4 Their diagnosis comes with actual math.

When the sampler picks a very low probability token, it ...
items.content:
/4 Their diagnosis comes with actual math.

When the sampler picks a very low probability token, it usually lands on a bad trajectory and earns negative advantage. The resulting gradient update grows the already dominant token most. So rare explorations end up sharpening the distribution further, which kills future exploration.

The fix is truncating the sampling tail with top-p. Starve that feedback loop and entropy stays high enough to keep learning.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal describes Cognition's diagnosis that low-probability token sampling sharpens the distribution and kills exploration, fixed by truncating the tail with top-p.

items.title:
/3 The interesting part is why extended RL usually stalls.

Long RL runs suffer entropy collapse. Th...
items.content:
/3 The interesting part is why extended RL usually stalls.

Long RL runs suffer entropy collapse. The policy sharpens, exploration dies, and reward flatlines within a few hundred steps. The model stops trying anything new, so there's nothing left to learn from.

Most teams treat that plateau as the ceiling. Cognition treated it as a bug.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal explains that extended RL usually stalls from entropy collapse, and Cognition treated the plateau as a fixable bug rather than a ceiling.

items.content:
/1 The setup first.

SWE-1.7 is the model behind Devin. Cognition took an open base that had already been RL trained by its original lab, then kept going with their own RL inside the real Devin harness on long software engineering tasks.

If RL gains saturate, stacking a second round on a saturated base should return almost nothing. That's the assumption this release pushes against.

items.title:
5/ Fable and GPT-5.6-Sol can score alike and still work differently.

This is a hands-on comparison,...
items.content:
5/ Fable and GPT-5.6-Sol can score alike and still work differently.

This is a hands-on comparison, labeled as vibes. Frame: one model as a thoughtful owl, the other as a rottweiler that will not let go.

In one benchmark-building test, the workhorse took six hours to two days and returned a thoroughly tested suite. The owl returned in about 40 minutes, twice, with vibe-based work, then self-scored at 100%.

A personal swear meter on Codex sessions fell from about 4-5% under GPT-5.5 to 1-2% under the workhorse, then jumped to about 7% going back.

> owl for architecture talk
> workhorse for list completion
> workhorse for multi-day goals
> use both on purpose

Plan and write with the owl. Execute with the workhorse.

Source: https://t.co/QwrJwwRcD9
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal shares a hands-on comparison framing Claude Fable and GPT-5.6-Sol as different work styles, recommending planning with one and execution with the other.
items.reasoning: Practical qualitative comparison of two frontier models with anecdotal metrics; low engagement and self-labeled as vibes, but useful practitioner framing.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal shares a hands-on comparison framing Claude Fable and GPT-5.6-Sol as different work styles, recommending planning with one and execution with the other.
items.content_html:
5/ Fable and GPT-5.6-Sol can score alike and still work differently.

This is a hands-on comparison, labeled as vibes. Frame: one model as a thoughtful owl, the other as a rottweiler that will not let go.

In one benchmark-building test, the workhorse took six hours to two days and returned a thoroughly tested suite. The owl returned in about 40 minutes, twice, with vibe-based work, then self-scored at 100%.

A personal swear meter on Codex sessions fell from about 4-5% under GPT-5.5 to 1-2% under the workhorse, then jumped to about 7% going back.

> owl for architecture talk

> workhorse for list completion

> workhorse for multi-day goals

> use both on purpose

Plan and write with the owl. Execute with the workhorse.

Source: https://t.co/QwrJwwRcD9

items.title:
3/ An agent is a while loop with four nested engineering layers.

This breakdown puts the model in t...
items.content:
3/ An agent is a while loop with four nested engineering layers.

This breakdown puts the model in the middle and stacks four zoom levels around it. The layers do not compete. Each one zooms further out.

Prompt engineering shapes one call: role, instructions, examples, format. 

Context engineering ranks everything in the turn, then cuts dead weight. 

Harness engineering is the code: tools, parse, retries, sub-agents, verifiers. 

Loop engineering runs many turns without a human re-prompting each step.

Stop cannot be the agent's word alone. It can report done while tests still fail.

> turn and token caps
> no-progress detectors
> separate completion checks
> checker off the maker

At the outer layer, you set the goal and stop rules up front, then let the run proceed.

Source: https://t.co/X4LfwN3zr9
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI

items.content:
/9 The takeaway.

A base model that already went through full RL post training absorbed a second RL run and improved by double digits. If that holds up outside Cognition's own evals, the post training ceiling was never a ceiling. It was an entropy problem with an engineering fix.

items.title:
/8 What to hold against it.

FrontierCode is Cognition's own benchmark. Terminal-Bench comparisons u...
items.content:
/8 What to hold against it.

FrontierCode is Cognition's own benchmark. Terminal-Bench comparisons use a different harness per vendor, so the head to head table isn't neutral ground. The weights are closed, and the model only runs inside Devin.

The base gain of 12.2 points is the claim that matters, and it's the one an outside team can't yet reproduce.

items.title:
/6 The infrastructure is its own story.

Inference rollouts run on clusters across three continents,...
items.content:
/6 The infrastructure is its own story.

Inference rollouts run on clusters across three continents, with weight updates shipped through cloud storage. A dead inference engine costs only its in flight sessions. Trainer state, the one fragile piece, checkpoints to local disk every step and replicates, so a failed node rebuilds in seconds instead of restarting the run.

items.title:
/5 Long tasks got a second trick.

When a rollout nears the context limit, the model summarizes its ...
items.content:
/5 Long tasks got a second trick.

When a rollout nears the context limit, the model summarizes its own working state and resumes from the summary. Effective horizon extends past the raw window.

Training alternates between phases too. Unconstrained phases optimize for task success. Budget phases penalize excess tokens, turns, and tool call time. Easy tasks get compressed, multi hour runs survive.

items.title: So many people are sending AI-written cover letters and résumés that it's easier than ever to stand ...
items.content:
So many people are sending AI-written cover letters and résumés that it's easier than ever to stand out with a genuine application.

If you just take the time to write something without asking an LLM, your application will stick out like a lighthouse in the middle of the night.

Unfortunately, 99% of you will read this, and will still go with "write me a cover letter, make no mistakes".

items.title:
/7 The behavior changed, not just the scores.

Compared to its base, SWE-1.7 reads more files and se...
items.content:
/7 The behavior changed, not just the scores.

Compared to its base, SWE-1.7 reads more files and searches the codebase more before editing anything. Its reasoning got denser, roughly half the words per sentence. The tradeoff Cognition admits is wider change scope. It touches more files and writes extra tests, whether you wanted them or not.

@alibaba_cloud researchers told me this week at ACL that the real action in the labs is building smaller models.

So the open source & “run locally” bulls will have their day.

items.title: create a really interesting game called "Don't Discuss Goblins" that should have some story elements...
items.content: create a really interesting game called "Don't Discuss Goblins" that should have some story elements but mostly be fun and fast moving - the goal is to avoid mentioning goblins, and this should be challenging. Dont make this just a glorified adventure game/text advanture but make sure it is expliclty about not mentioning goblins even though you really want to. Generate 100 ideas for games first. Pick the best 5. Get feedback from agents with different personas about which of the 5 to pursue and then spend time getting the mechanics right before building, including doing any prototyping. You should lushly illustrate the game as needed in whatever style you think is good, creating images and sprites as needed using GPT-imaggen or your native image generator. you need to playtest the game as a player, not just for technical use. there should be a good core game and mechanic.

items.title:
We ask AI to make decisions faster and cleaner than humans, but speed does not remove uncertainty. 
...
items.content:
We ask AI to make decisions faster and cleaner than humans, but speed does not remove uncertainty. 

People remain responsible for deciding when the data is good enough, when more depth is needed, and when action can no longer wait. 

Microblog  by  @antgrasso  #HumanCenteredAI 

The real tension is not between humans and AI.

It is between acting too late and acting without enough understanding.

Some decisions need speed because delay increases exposure. Others need deeper analysis because the impact is high or the context is still unclear.

AI can help by comparing signals, showing patterns, and reviewing outcomes over time. But it still depends on data quality and clear criteria before its outputs can guide action.

Teams should define in advance what “good enough” means for each decision: the level of evidence required, who validates the result, and when human judgment must take the lead.

AI supports better decisions when it helps people manage timing and interpretation, without pretending that every answer is complete.
items.author: @antgrasso
items.summary: A human-centered AI microblog arguing that speed does not remove uncertainty, humans remain responsible for deciding when data is good enough, and teams should predefine what good enough means for each decision.

items.content:
@icmlconf QuantumLean-Bench appears at the @icmlconf  2026 AI4Physics Workshop and evaluates quantum-science reasoning with both informal explanations and Lean-oriented formal outputs. 

Quantum reasoning is a strong testbed for AI4Science because it combines physics, linear algebra, probability, circuits, states, operators, and domain-specific notation. The benchmark asks whether models can move from natural-language reasoning toward structured, machine-checkable scientific representations.

items.title: @icmlconf ITPEval appears at the @icmlconf  2026 AI4Math Workshop and studies formal translation acr...
items.content:
@icmlconf ITPEval appears at the @icmlconf  2026 AI4Math Workshop and studies formal translation across Lean 4, Rocq/Coq, Isabelle/HOL, and HOL Light. 

The goal is not just to translate syntax, but to test whether models preserve mathematical meaning across different foundations, libraries, theorem names, tactics, and proof styles. We also release the infrastructure to evaluate these systems across multiple theorem provers, making it easier to benchmark progress on prover interoperability.

Paper: https://t.co/o9Lzx4gMz2

items.title: @icmlconf BRIDGE appears at the @icmlconf  2026 AI4Math Workshop and studies verified program synthe...
items.content:
@icmlconf BRIDGE appears at the @icmlconf  2026 AI4Math Workshop and studies verified program synthesis in Lean. 

It is complementary to broader program-verification work, but uses Lean itself as the functional programming language, specification language, and proof environment. This lets us connect generated programs, formal specs, theorem statements, and machine-checkable proofs in one setting, moving beyond test-passing code toward functional correctness.

items.content:
TorchLean was selected as a Spotlight at the @icmlconf  2026 AI4Math Workshop. 

TorchLean covers typed tensors, graph programs, autograd, floating-point semantics, verification/certificate checking, PyTorch interop, and examples across ML and scientific ML.

It is exciting to see people start using it to verify ML systems, and we have continued expanding the framework every week with more proofs, features, examples, and runtime support.

items.title: Excited to share four Lean-related papers from our group at @icmlconf workshops in Math and Physics!...
items.content:
Excited to share four Lean-related papers from our group at @icmlconf workshops in Math and Physics!

Together, these works explore how theorem proving can support verified ML systems, functional program synthesis, interoperability across proof assistants, and scientific reasoning. A common theme is using Lean not just as a proof assistant for mathematics, but as infrastructure for building, specifying, checking, and evaluating AI systems. 

@Robertljg, Jennifer Cruden, Will Adkisson, UIUC team (Xiangru Zhong, @huan_zhang12), @AmazonScience Team (Carson Eisenach, @udayaghai, Dominique Perrault-Joncas, Dean Foster), Jiayi Wu, Isha Goswami, and Anushka Paulchoudhury. @Caltech 
#ICML2026 #AI4Math #AI4Physics #Lean #FormalVerification #AI4Science #MachineLearning

items.title:
I have been trying to find something meaningful to say about the Id Software layoffs.

My “Microsoft...
items.content:
I have been trying to find something meaningful to say about the Id Software layoffs.

My “Microsoft will probably be a good steward of the brand” statement isn’t aging well, and this is certainly going to dampen the mood of the founder reunion at QuakeCon next month.

I’m saddened, but I can’t muster anger or outrage over it. I don’t have access to the books, but I suspect that Id Software  was a marginal business from Microsoft’s perspective. I believe the reports that Minecraft revenues have been carrying several other studios.

To continue being produced long term, games need to succeed, not just be beloved.

Games are competing with every other option for spending your leisure time and money, and the competition is brutal.

You can’t rule out the possibility that executives are idiots, but that shouldn’t be your default belief. I don’t think there is any obvious path that would have doubled the revenue from Id games.

Could they have gotten more with a different pricing strategy?

Could they have created more things for fans to buy?

Could they have cost effectively marketed in a way that reached more players that would have loved and bought the games?

Could they have changed the game designs and broadened the appeal to more players without alienating existing ones?

Could they have produced the games at a lower cost, faster or cheaper?

I really don’t know.

The game isn’t over yet, and I hope the studio rallies through.

items.title: Anyone still mooing that generative AI is somehow a revolution in machines and "we must learn to coe...
items.content:
Anyone still mooing that generative AI is somehow a revolution in machines and "we must learn to coexist with machines" is an idiot, and at the age of this guy, this isn't curable.

Truly, for the last decade, we have been witnessing proof that public servicemen must have a strict age limit.

items.title: Feynman Lectures on Computation (Frontiers in Physics. Anniversary Edition): https://t.co/7XVVTczFC2...
items.content:
Feynman Lectures on Computation (Frontiers in Physics. Anniversary Edition): https://t.co/7XVVTczFC2

Amazon summary: "The last lecture course that Nobel Prize winner Richard P. Feynman gave to students at Caltech from 1983 to 1986 was not on physics but on computer science. The first edition of the Feynman Lectures on Computation, published in 1996, provided an overview of standard and not-so-standard topics in computer science given in Feynman’s inimitable style. Although now over 20 years old, most of the material is still relevant and interesting, and Feynman’s unique philosophy of learning and discovery shines through. For this new edition, Tony Hey has updated the lectures with an invited chapter from Professor John Preskill on 'Quantum Computing 40 Years Later'. This contribution captures the progress made toward building a quantum computer since Feynman’s original suggestions in 1981."
items.author: @KirkDBorne
items.summary: A share of the Feynman Lectures on Computation, noting an added chapter on quantum computing 40 years later.
items.reasoning: Well-regarded computing/quantum resource share with solid engagement and useful context.
items.summary_html: A share of the Feynman Lectures on Computation, noting an added chapter on quantum computing 40 years later.

items.title: Gun Projectile Engineering - Flight Dynamics Anatomy and Aerodynamic Trajectory Engineering - #Mathe...
items.content:
Gun Projectile Engineering - Flight Dynamics Anatomy and Aerodynamic Trajectory Engineering - #Mathematics 

Hey there! I'm heading to the vending machine. Want anything? Soda, gum, or perhaps a round or two of bullets for your rifle? Typically, purchasing gun ammunition requires completing a background check form, which is reviewed by the gun merchant for approval before allowing you to buy the ammunition. 

Apparently, there are now AI-powered vending machines in some supermarkets in Alabama, Oklahoma, and Texas that are selling the ammunition. These machines, created by a company called American Rounds who have 100+ followers on Instagram, claim to use AI technology and facial recognition software to verify the identity and age of the buyer before dispensing the ammunition. It seems like a simple process to run through the machine. Just provide identification, let the camera scan your face, and if everything checks out, you can purchase the bullets. 

By the way, these machines are referred to as smart retail automated ammo dispensers by American Rounds. "Our smart retail automated ammo dispensers," states the company's website, "are equipped with advanced AI technology, card scanning, and facial recognition software." The website emphasizes that American Rounds leads the way in retail ammunition technology, continuously integrating the latest AI and machine learning advancement. These vending machines are working 24/7 regardless of a public holiday website reads. 

A bullet is a precision assembly: Meplat tip, copper jacket, lead core, cannelure, boattail base. Each part is engineered to control aerodynamic drag and accuracy at distance. It's not a simple metal https://t.co/2lWWjT3D9e flight, a spinning bullet precesses and nutates like a gyroscope. Researchers measured precession at 298–964 rad/s & nutation at 2,635–3,302 rad/s for a 9mm round. Stable flight requires a gyroscopic stability coefficient. At supersonic speeds, schlieren research reveals shockwaves, bow shocks & Mach cones around the cartridge. At impact, it creates both a permanent wound cavity and a larger cavitation zone through tissue or muscle.

items.content:

Top 20 AI Concepts You Should Know:

1. Machine Learning: Core algorithms, statistics, and model training techniques.

2. Deep Learning: Hierarchical neural networks learning complex representations automatically.

3. Neural Networks: Layered architectures efficiently model nonlinear relationships accurately.

4. NLP: Techniques to process and understand natural language text.

5. Computer Vision: Algorithms interpreting and analyzing visual data effectively

6. Reinforcement Learning: Distributed traffic across multiple servers for reliability.

7. Generative Models: Creating new data samples using learned data.

8. LLM: Generates human-like text using massive pre-trained data.

9. Transformers: Self-attention-based architecture powering modern AI models.

10. Feature Engineering: Designing informative features to improve model performance significantly.

11. Supervised Learning: Learns useful representations without labeled data.

12. Bayesian Learning: Incorporate uncertainty using probabilistic model approaches.

13. Prompt Engineering: Crafting effective inputs to guide generative model outputs.

14. AI Agents: Autonomous systems that perceive, decide, and act.

15. Fine-Tuning Models: Customizes pre-trained models for domain-specific tasks.

16. Multimodal Models: Processes and generates across multiple data types like images, videos, and text.

17. Embeddings: Transforms input into machine-readable vector formats.

18. Vector Search: Finds similar items using dense vector embeddings.

19. Model Evaluation: Assessing predictive performance using validation techniques.

20. AI Infrastructure: Deploying scalable systems to support AI operations.

items.title: The concepts important to understanding the ways in which to manage component and architectural know...
items.content:
The concepts important to understanding the ways in which to manage component and architectural knowledge, strategic management, strategic innovation in an organization. Work by Abernathy and Utterback (1978) , Rosenberg (1982), Clark (1985), and Sahal (1986) and evidence from studies of several industries show that product technologies do not emerge fully developed at the outset of their commercial lives in organization.  Most of the research and development conducted by the firms for technical evaluation of innovation is usually characterized by applied research with greater periods of the initial phase realization.  The other concept is organizations build the competence range of activities and capability around the recurrent tasks that perform the innovation.  Most of these tasks come to an end with the emergence of the design. A dominant design can reach economies of scale or to take advantage of the externalities.

An organization’s communication channels and strategies build the architectural knowledge.  The communication channels will come to embody its architectural knowledge of the linkages between components that are critical to the effective design. The information filters of an organization also embody its architectural knowledge.

The entrepreneurial firms can use architectural knowledge of a technical system or component to gain strategic advantage, first by identifying the ‘bottlenecks,’ and then by creating a new architecture that isolates the bottlenecks in modules.  The firm pursuing this strategy will have a higher return on invested capital (ROIC) than competitors with a less modular design.  Over time, the focal firm can drive the ROIC of competitors below their cost of capital, causing them to shrink and possibly exit the market.  This strategy was used by Sun Microsystems in the 1980s and Dell Computer in 1990s, NVIDIA in 2000s. My initial research in 2015 in semiconductor industry with NVIDIA's integration strategy was prescient, but more than a decade has produced far richer empirical material. The semiconductor and AI infrastructure ecosystems have evolved from a simple fabless vs IDM diagram into a multi-layer architecture of nested bottlenecks, each of which represents next era of semiconductor value creation. The initial research I’ve conducted was about strategic innovation from (Henderson–Clark, 1990) provides the correct theoretical spine: architectural knowledge, module interfaces, and bottleneck isolation are the mechanisms through which firms generate above-cost-of-capital returns (ROIC). Every modern case study below validates that model with fresh, quantifiable evidence.

items.title:
The next board-level AI question cannot only be:

“What use cases are we funding?”

It also has to b...
items.content:
The next board-level AI question cannot only be:

“What use cases are we funding?”

It also has to be:

“Is our infrastructure prepared to support those systems across demanding environments?”

As businesses continue scaling AI and automation, resilient connectivity is becoming increasingly important for operational visibility and connected workflows.

I wrote the full piece on why connectivity resilience is becoming a bigger part of AI infrastructure strategy, and how organizations are approaching resilient connectivity across distributed environments.

Read it here: https://t.co/g0jokV0yCF 

How are you thinking about connectivity resilience as AI operations continue to scale?

T-Mobile for Business Partner #SuperBroadband

items.title:
Most companies have scaled cloud infrastructure and AI initiatives.

But many organizations are now ...
items.content:
Most companies have scaled cloud infrastructure and AI initiatives.

But many organizations are now reevaluating another important layer of modern operations: connectivity resilience.

As connected systems become more distributed, resilient network infrastructure is becoming increasingly important for operational visibility and workflow coordination.

I explored this at the @TMobileBusiness SuperBroadband launch. A thread.

T-Mobile for Business Partner

items.content_html:
Most companies have scaled cloud infrastructure and AI initiatives.

But many organizations are now reevaluating another important layer of modern operations: connectivity resilience.

As connected systems become more distributed, resilient network infrastructure is becoming increasingly important for operational visibility and workflow coordination.

I explored this at the @TMobileBusiness SuperBroadband launch. A thread.

T-Mobile for Business Partner

items.title:
AI Army Phalanx

The US military has shifted its focus from the long-standing pursuit of powered arm...
items.content:
AI Army Phalanx

The US military has shifted its focus from the long-standing pursuit of powered armor to the development of an army militant. HEO (Hyper enabled operator) - a cutting-edge tactical AI assistant designed to support special operations forces. 

As day transitions to night, American special operators find themselves on the edge. Deployed in a densely populated urban area within a politically volatile region, they observe a surge in local activity, with streets and markets bustling more than usual. While intelligence indicates a high threat level, details remain unclear, compelling the team to keep a low profile to avoid attracting hostile elements. To assess potential threats, the Americans opt for a cautious approach, choosing to blend in with potential crowds and forgo conspicuous tactical gear. An operator ventures into the neighborhood's main thoroughfare, keen to gather crucial intelligence.

At the push of a button, the operator gains access to a wealth of information. An array of sensors linked to his head-up display begins gathering data from the surrounding environment, capturing body language, heart rates, facial expressions, and snippets of local conversations in various dialects. This data is swiftly transmitted to his backpack supercomputers for processing, assisted by an onboard artificial intelligence engine. The processed information is promptly relayed back to the head-up display, providing a clear assessment from the operators' tactical AI companion: The bustling activity is attributed to an upcoming series of seasonal events, with most individuals displaying excitement and cheer, posing minimal threat to the team. Crisis averted, at least for the time being.

In recent years, Defense Department officials have frequently presented this as one of the many potential scenarios when discussing the future of US special operations forces. These elite troops are entrusted with confronting some of the world's most intricate threats as the tip of the spear of the US military. Defense officials have envisioned a future of warfare involving brain implants, or powered armor reminiscent of Starship Troopers. US Special Operations Command projects that the next generation of armed conflict would revolve around a relatively straightforward concept: The HEO.

Initially introduced to the public in 2019 through an essay penned by officials from SOCOM’s Joint Acquisition Task Force (JATF) for Small Wars Journal, the hyper-enabled operator (HEO) concept serves as the follow-up program to the Tactical Assault Light Operator Suit (TALOS) initiative. Launched in 2013, TALOS aimed to equip US special operations forces with an iron suit following the 2012 death of a Navy SEAL during a hostage rescue operation in Afghanistan. This ambitious project sought to enhance operators’ survivability in combat by rendering them virtually immune to small-arms fire through advanced armor layers, marking the latest chapter in the Pentagon's long-standing endeavor to develop a powered exoskeleton for infantry troops. Although the TALOS effort was discontinued in 2019 due to challenges in integrating its heterogeneous systems into a unified unit, the insights gained from the program laid the groundwork for the emergence of HEO as a natural successor.

The primary goal of the HEO concept is clear-cut: to provide warfighters with cognitive overmatch on the battlefield, enabling them to dominate the situation by making informed decisions faster than the opponent, as described by SOCOM officials. Rather than solely emphasizing physical enhancements such as next-generation body armor and advanced weaponry for US special operations forces, the future operator will be equipped with technologies designed to elevate their situational awareness and decision-making to surpass the adversary's capabilities. Former fighter pilot and Air Force colonel John Boyd introduced the "OODA loop" (observe, orient, decide, act) as the fundamental military decision-making model for the 21st century; the HEO concept aims to leverage technology to tighten that loop to the extent that operators are truly making more astute and rapid decisions than the enemy. 

In pursuit of this objective the HEO concept entails installing the powered armor central to the TALOS initiative with sophisticated communications equipment and a robust sensor suite, leveraging advanced computing architecture. This setup enables the operator to gather pertinent data and transform it into actionable information through a user friendly interface such as a head-up display, all while operating at the edge, in locations where conventional communication networks may not be accessible. 

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #LLM #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode  

References

Keller, J. (2024, July 8). AI-powered super soldiers are more than just a pipe dream. WIRED. Retrieved from https://t.co/LZ12xSVTE5

Manson, K. (2026, March 23). Omniscience, omnipresence, and omnipotence: Meet the gods of AI warfare. WIRED. Retrieved from https://t.co/oJJPxZ3b8O

items.title:
Here is what this means for AI strategy specifically.

Your AI models need operational data. Your cl...
items.content:
Here is what this means for AI strategy specifically.

Your AI models need operational data. Your cloud applications rely on resilient connectivity. Your payments, robotics, analytics, and field operations all depend on connected infrastructure.

When connectivity becomes inconsistent, operational visibility and workflow coordination can become more difficult to manage.

It can slow decision-making and impact how connected systems operate across distributed environments.

The AI may be well designed. The infrastructure may be sophisticated. But resilient network infrastructure is becoming increasingly important for supporting AI-enabled operations.

items.title:
Let me challenge a belief most IT leaders hold.

“We have a backup ISP. We’re protected.”

But busin...
items.content:
Let me challenge a belief most IT leaders hold.

“We have a backup ISP. We’re protected.”

But businesses are realizing connectivity resilience can become more complicated across distributed environments.

If multiple connections rely on similar infrastructure dependencies, disruptions can still impact connected operations and workflows.

That’s why more organizations are reevaluating how they approach resilient connectivity infrastructure.

items.title:
The big takeaway:

As AI becomes more integrated into business operations, connectivity infrastructu...
items.content:
The big takeaway:

As AI becomes more integrated into business operations, connectivity infrastructure is becoming increasingly important for supporting connected systems and workflows.

I filmed this with @TMobileBusiness at the SuperBroadband launch to explore how integrated connectivity can support resilient operations across demanding environments.

Watch the full video: https://t.co/r9wGuiujUT 

Where do you think resilient connectivity will matter most first — factories, logistics, energy, or somewhere else?

T-Mobile for Business Partner #SuperBroadband

items.title: I just watched AI systems & cloud apps continue operating after connectivity was intentionally inter...
items.content:
I just watched AI systems & cloud apps continue operating after connectivity was intentionally interrupted during the @TMobileBusiness SuperBroadband demo.

That’s when it clicked:

AI systems depend heavily on resilient connectivity infrastructure.

Roger Billings, Principal Solution Architect, T-Mobile for Business, walked me through SuperBroadband and how integrated connectivity can support operational resilience across demanding environments.

Here’s the breakdown…

T-Mobile for Business Partner

This matters most where operational disruptions can be costly.

Think:

→ manufacturing plants
→ logistics hubs
→ energy sites
→ rural operations
→ AI-powered monitoring
→ autonomous workflows

As businesses scale connected systems and AI-enabled operations, resilient connectivity infrastructure is becoming increasingly important for operational visibility and workflow coordination.

items.title:
The key takeaway is not simply backup connectivity.

It’s how integrated connectivity infrastructure...
items.content:
The key takeaway is not simply backup connectivity.

It’s how integrated connectivity infrastructure can support operational visibility and connected workflows across demanding environments.

During the demo, we saw how multiple connectivity technologies worked together to support connected operations.

That matters as more businesses scale AI-enabled systems, automation, and distributed workflows.

items.title:
The traditional backup connectivity model is evolving.

Businesses today still face challenges from:...
items.content:
The traditional backup connectivity model is evolving.

Businesses today still face challenges from:

→ cable cuts
→ storms
→ tower outages
→ rural connectivity gaps
→ operational disruptions

In the demo, we explored how SuperBroadband from @TMobileBusiness integrates T-Mobile’s 5G network and Starlink connectivity to help support resilient operations across distributed environments.

items.title: As a scientist, I love when facts challenge our preconceived notions. Here's a counterintuitive insi...
items.content:
As a scientist, I love when facts challenge our preconceived notions. Here's a counterintuitive insight for marketing teams: “tighter budgets can lead to better content.” 📊

As budgets shrink and briefs get more restrictive, brands may start leaning harder on the creative freedom of the influencers they work with. In fact, when you can't out-spend, you need to out-create — and that means real co-creation, not rigid briefs. 🎨

See my perspective (and many others) in @SpotlightAR’s new 2026 B2B Influencer Marketing Report — right here:
… https://t.co/KSv5LciuMx 👈
———
#B2BInfluence #InfluencerMarketing #CoCreation
items.author: @KirkDBorne
items.summary: A post arguing tighter budgets can yield better content through influencer co-creation, promoting a 2026 B2B influencer marketing report.
items.reasoning: Marketing self-promotion loosely framed as insight; low relevance to AI/ML.
items.summary_html: A post arguing tighter budgets can yield better content through influencer co-creation, promoting a 2026 B2B influencer marketing report.
items.content_html:
